result:
Lately I’ve been thinking about the way light changes during the day—how it’s not just illumination but information. At dawn the light is tentative, almost embarrassed to be seen, a blush on the horizon that apologizes for waking you. By midday it becomes a prosecutor, cross-examining every surface, revealing the dust you missed, the unfinished edges of your life. Then evening arrives and light turns merciful, softening the world’s textures the way memory softens regrets, making even the ugliest buildings look like they’re keeping gentle secrets.

I keep noticing how we’re allergic to stillness. Phones have become the new smoking—something to do with our hands when we feel exposed just standing there. Waiting rooms, elevator rides, the fragile moments between courses at dinner: we fill them like we’re caulking cracks that might let the void seep in. But the void was already here. It’s the negative space that gives shape to everything else, like how the silence between notes lets music mean something more than noise.

Yesterday I watched a child methodically dismantle a dandelion clock, counting “one-Mississippi, two-Mississippi” between each scattered seed. Her seriousness was devotional. She wasn’t destroying time; she was distributing it, turning one future into dozens of smaller futures that might land anywhere. I wanted to tell her that this is what we do with stories, with love, with grief—take the dense center of something and release it to the wind, trusting that some fragment might root in alien soil. But she already knew. Children always know until we teach them not to.

There’s a kind of holiness in unfinished things. The half-written letter that never mailed itself. The sweater sleeves that remain wristless, keeping their potential coiled in a basket. My neighbor’s 1973 Corvette on blocks in his garage, its engine parts arranged like a metallic constellation across the concrete, waiting for a resurrection that’s been promised for fifteen years. These suspended possibilities are more truthful than completions; they acknowledge that we live in the middle of the story, that the clock is always one tick away from the next sentence.

Sometimes I think we’re all just elaborate systems for converting light into narrative. Photons strike retina, become impulse, become language, become the thing you tell yourself at 3 AM about who you are. The universe experiencing itself in the dark, making up bedtime stories about how the stars came to be, never quite remembering that we are the stars, temporarily assembled into the shape of loneliness.

The most radical thing we could do might be to treat small things as if they were large. To butter toast like it mattered. To fold laundry like origami cranes. To meet eyes with the grocery clerk as if you were exchanging ancient passwords. We keep waiting for scale, for enormity, but the revolution might be microscopic—a different way of noticing how the morning light makes your coffee cup look like it’s holding a small sun, how that could be enough to dismantle every empire that ever taught you to feel small.

I’m learning to mistrust the word “later.” It’s a velvet-lined trap. Later is where we banish the versions of ourselves we’re too polite to kill outright. The books we’ll write when we have time. The apologies we’ll make when we’re braver. The gardens we’ll plant when the soil of our lives is less cluttered with the weeds of obligation. But the soil is always cluttered. The brave moment is now, when your hands are still dirty from whatever you’ve been using them to survive.

Tonight the moon looks like a chalk mark on a prison wall, counting days we’ve forgotten we’re serving. But even that is a story I’m telling. The moon is just the moon, neither sad nor free, practicing its ancient dentistry on the dark. It’s we who do the time, we who parole ourselves with every breath, we who keep pardoning the world for not being what we thought it would, keep showing up to the parole hearings of our own hearts, surprised to find the gates have been open all along.
